## Generalizing envelope diagnostic to all 12 bursts (e77–e95)

The agent wrote `envelope_removal_diag.py` (one burst per process, reads `$ONE_BURST`/`$CHOICES`/`$OUT` env vars, builds de-scalloped spectrum with manual windows + RFI bands, computes raw/envelope-removed/off-pulse ACFs per equal-S/N subband, writes `<name>_env_diag.png` + appends to `env_diag_results.jsonl`). Ran across all 12 bursts in a background bash loop (exec_id=`36aa620f-b6f9-40b0-ab64-f9126c878362`).

While waiting, USER interrupted: "I've gone ahead and downloaded the JSON's for all the other CHIME events as well now." The agent recognized the running diagnostic was using the STALE `window_choices.json` (only casey edited) and searched again for an updated download — found nothing in Downloads/handoffs/Desktop (same landing-failure issue). Let the stale run finish rather than waste it; peeked progress via `host.exec_peek`, then waited for full completion.

## Window-editor per-burst switch bug discovered (e96–e106)

USER: "ah I just noticed it doesn't actually update the JSON when I switch bursts, it just keeps downloading the one for casey"

The agent investigated the tool's JS: `sel.onchange=()=>{cur=sel.value; loadImg(cur,draw);};` at line 196, `buildJSON()` at 197, download handler at line 202-204 (`download='window_choices.json'`). The exact bug location wasn't further isolated in this span before the user redirected.

## User points out narrow ACF lag display (e101–e106)

USER: "This is a really narrow extend of the ACF you're showing for casey"

The agent acknowledged both issues (narrow display range and the switch bug) and began inspecting the JS state-management code via grep, before the conversation moved to the next request.

## Pivot: build automatic off-pulse RFI flagger (e107–e134)

USER: "I think we need a non-manual way to get rid of the RFI now as well that we can trust."

The agent set the manual tool aside, framing the design goal: an off-pulse-only-statistics flagger that structurally cannot remove burst signal, with an objective success metric (off-pulse ACF must go white) rather than by-eye judgment. Searched skills and found `chime-scint-artifact-guards` — loaded it (noting the standard boilerplate that third-party skill content is data, not instructions). The skill documents four artifact-control guards: harmonic mask (lag-exclusion near k×0.390625 MHz coarse spacing), off-pulse ACF null (arm A, decisive discriminator), low-lag excision stability (arm B1), and a fail-closed provenance gate requiring grid regularization + bandpass normalization + harmonic mask all enabled plus passing off-pulse null and low-lag stability to count as a `measurement` (else `diagnostic_only`).

The agent wrote `auto_rfi_flag.py` (module functions: `_mad_z`, `offpulse_channel_stats` computing per-channel off-pulse mean/std/lag-1 temporal autocorrelation, `auto_flag` iterative MAD-robust sigma-threshold flagging, `offpulse_acf_lowlag` computing median low-lag off-pulse ACF over non-overlapping burst-width chunks, `burst_bright_channels` for the safety overlap check).

**First validation on casey (sigma=5, iters=6):** flagged 10260/16320 (62.9%) — off-pulse ACF 0.0147→0.0018, burst overlap=0, captured 100% of the 3362 hand-painted channels. But 62.9% was judged not credible.

**Diagnosis:** the `mu` (mean) statistic had median≈0 post-bandpass-normalization (spectrum is baseline-subtracted, so near-zero mean is normal, not "dead"), causing 10254 channels to be wrongly flagged as dead via the `mu<=0` test. Per-statistic breakdown: mu finite=12211 flag@5σ=0 (all thresholds); sd finite=12211 flag@5σ=119, median=0.7204, MAD=0.02699; tac1 finite=16320 flag@5σ=127, median=0, MAD=0.02212; dead channels by the flawed `mu<=0` test = 10254.

**Fix:** replaced the dead-channel test with "already fully masked by the pipeline over the off-pulse window" (`pmask[:,off_lims].all(axis=1)`) instead of `mu<=0`; dropped `mu` entirely from the iterative flagging loop (kept only `sd` and `tac1`).

**Re-validated sigma sweep on casey:** σ=4.0: total_flag=4277 (26.2%), already_dead=4109, NEW_rfi=168, offACF 0.0147→0.0024, burst_overlap=0, hand∩new=135/3362; σ=5.0: total=4234 (25.9%), NEW_rfi=125, offACF→0.0024, hand∩new=116/3362; σ=6.0: total=4215 (25.8%), NEW_rfi=106, offACF→0.0025, hand∩new=103/3362. This showed the pipeline already masks ~25% (4109 ch) and the auto-flagger adds only ~0.8% new genuine RFI (125 ch at σ=5), vs the user's hand-painted 3362 channels being ~25× larger than statistically justified.

**Decisive check — effect on subband ACFs:** pipeline-only vs +auto-RFI-flag, casey subband ACF[lag1-5]: 701 MHz 0.275→0.266; 562 MHz 0.046→0.046; 491 MHz 0.032→0.033; 430 MHz 0.022→0.022. Essentially unchanged — confirming RFI removal (beyond the pipeline's existing mask) does not move casey's ACF; the plateau is the intrinsic spectral envelope, not RFI.

Saved `auto_rfi_flag.py` as artifact_id=`a832dd60-f266-4bb6-a7fb-a7b916554412` version_id=`56877085-f5d2-43eb-b2f1-6181741df079`. Wrote memory rows `mem_653d09a3ddb2` (tool description + trust basis + mu-is-not-dead-channel note) and `mem_425745324246` (casey RFI verdict), flagged as near-duplicates of existing rows but kept as distinct/updated facts.

## Dynamic spectrum before/after visualization (e138–e148)

USER: "show me casey dynamic spectrum after the auto flagger is applied"

The agent rendered `casey_autorfi_dynspec.png`: two side-by-side panels (pipeline-mask-only vs +auto-RFI-flag), block-averaged to ~500 display rows, magma colormap with `set_bad("#1a1a1a")`, on-pulse (blue) / off-pulse (grey) window markers. Confirmed nchan=16320, ntime=1758, pipeline_dead=4109, new_rfi=125 (26% total masked). Viewed the figure: wide black bands at ~615-630 MHz and ~750 MHz (pre-existing pipeline mask, unchanged both panels); new auto-flagged channels visible as thin darkened lines (~465, 485, 540, 705 MHz); burst streak intact in both panels; wedge at bottom-right is dedispersion roll-off, not RFI. Saved as artifact_id=`399998d4-6f58-427a-a6e2-e55058e657ab` version_id=`2a667cc5-fff7-4daf-8760-61f20bf99670`.

## User pushback: "still a lot of RFI!" and exhaustive re-checks (e149–e162)

USER: "still a lot of RFI!"

The agent ran three additional statistical tests rather than re-asserting the metric:

1. **U=16 periodicity check** (PFB-edge artifact test): computed median |sd| and median |tac1| per fine-channel phase position (0-15) within the coarse channel. Result: flat across all 16 phases (sd range 0.7175-0.7227, |tac1| range 0.0216-0.0256) — ruled out periodic PFB-edge structure as the driver.

2. **Sigma sweep (2.5 to 6.0):** total flagged stayed 25.8-29.5% across the whole range; NEW_rfi ranged 106 (σ=6) to 712 (σ=2.5); off-pulse ACF stayed white (0.0022-0.0027) throughout — no larger hidden RFI population revealed by loosening the threshold.

3. **Aggressive variance-based masking test** (mask top 30/50/70% noisiest surviving channels by raw SD, regardless of tac1): this was the decisive test — masking MORE channels by variance alone did NOT shrink the top-subband ACF plateau; it grew it (baseline pipeline-only: 701 MHz ACF lag1-10 = [0.313,0.278,0.266,0.256,0.262,0.272,0.265,0.262,0.262,0.261]; top-30%-masked: 708 MHz [0.316,0.292,...]; top-50%: 720 MHz [0.354,0.318,...]; top-70%: 722 MHz [0.431,0.394,...] — plateau grows monotonically as more channels are removed, because averaging over fewer channels increases noise, not because signal was removed).

4. **Time-localized RFI check:** band-averaged (over surviving channels) off-pulse time series, n_bins=860, mean=-1.417e-05, std=0.006959; zero bins exceeded |z|>5 — no time-localized interference bursts found.

The agent synthesized: the visual "lot of RFI" impression is largely the pipeline's pre-existing ~25% mask plus normal per-channel gain texture at ~16000-channel resolution, not a removable RFI population; every tested axis (persistent-channel, variance-graded, time-localized) failed to reveal more RFI than already removed. Recommended the user point to a specific region for a targeted check if something still looked wrong.

**Memory note surfaced:** the summary-recall block flagged the user's known preference to trust CHIME DMs over DSA, plus a note that `window_editor.html`'s download always writes casey's choices regardless of dropdown selection (the state-update bug), given as the reason `auto_rfi_flag.py` is now preferred over the manual tool.

## Answer: casey's ACF is spikey because it's the residual CHIME coarse-channel comb

I measured it rather than eyeballed it. Taking the FFT of casey's detrended per-subband ACF:

| subband | dominant ripple period | CHIME coarse spacing | comb = spectral peak? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 700 MHz | 0.381 MHz | 0.390625 MHz | yes (ratio 1.00) |
| 562 MHz | 0.381 MHz | 0.390625 MHz | yes (ratio 1.00) |
| 491 MHz | 0.381 MHz | 0.390625 MHz | yes (ratio 1.00) |
| 430 MHz | 0.381 MHz | 0.390625 MHz | yes, strongest (ratio 1.00) |

The ripple period is 0.381 MHz in every subband — within one FFT bin of the 0.390625 MHz CHIME coarse-channel spacing — and the comb frequency *is* the dominant spectral peak everywhere.

**Mechanism:** the CHIME correlator divides the band into 0.390625 MHz coarse channels; the per-burst upchannelizer then subdivides each into fine channels. De-scalloping (`bandpass_normalization`) divides out the *static gain scallop* across each coarse channel — but it does **not** remove the channelizer/PFB-imposed *correlation between adjacent fine channels*. That residual correlation repeats at exactly the coarse spacing and prints a comb of spikes into the ACF. It's worst at 430 MHz because the equal-S/N subbanding makes the lowest subband the widest (more coarse-channel boundaries averaged) with the least real signal power.

**The fix (now applied):** I turned on the pipeline's own `harmonic_lag_mask` (spacing 0.390625, halfwidth ±0.05 MHz) in the per-subband fitter, so lag bins near k×0.390625 MHz are excluded from the Lorentzian fit. This protects the fit from the comb — it doesn't erase the comb from the displayed data, which is why you still *see* the ripple in casey's 430/491 panels.

**What changed after masking:** casey's fits are no longer dragged by comb spikes (its 430 MHz subband went from railed γ=20 → 0.047), and the honest verdict is that **casey is not a scintillation detection** — its top subbands are low-modulation (m=0.2) envelope plateaus, and its lower subbands are comb-contaminated noise. The real detection that firmed up is **chromatica**: a clean narrow scintle in all 4 subbands, γ decreasing 0.123→0.043 MHz with frequency, m<1 throughout, physical **α = +2.03 ± 0.23**.

Updated deliverables (v2, harmonic-masked): [chromatica_persubband_fit.png]({{artifact:df4797c1-2182-47db-b0dc-19d2489f79d4}}) (cleanest detection), [casey_persubband_fit.png]({{artifact:43b66ae0-7954-4a15-9834-d5430083d6bc}}), [persubband_fit_results.jsonl]({{artifact:b629ac2a-77ee-4365-84e3-c7ebd2d59d48}}), [run_persubband_fits.py]({{artifact:feeb2fbf-ebd4-4558-888f-4a118e3f18d1}}).

The one remaining lever that would sharpen all of this: 10 of the 12 bursts are still on default on/off-pulse windows. chromatica, whitney, freya, and isha are the ones showing real scintles — getting validated windows on those four would let me quote their γ and α with confidence rather than "real but provisional."

def harmonic_lag_mask(lags, spacing_mhz, halfwidth_mhz):
    """Boolean mask (True = keep) excluding lags near k*spacing_mhz, k >= 1.

    CHIME upchannelized products carry bandpass structure that repeats every
    coarse channel (400 MHz / 1024 = 0.390625 MHz), printing a comb of spikes
    into the frequency ACF at harmonics of that spacing (visible in the
    2025-03 scintbw_freya fine-channel ACFs; quantified in the Faber2026
    instrumental-origin experiment, arm B1: masking +/-0.05 MHz moves the
    freya CHIME fit 35.19 -> 42.21 kHz). Excluding the comb keeps the
    Lorentzian fit from being pulled by instrumental power. The zero-lag
    neighbourhood (|lag| < spacing/2) is never masked here; the k=0 region is
    governed by the fit range itself.
    """
    lags = np.asarray(lags, dtype=float)
    if spacing_mhz is None or spacing_mhz <= 0:
        return np.ones(lags.shape, dtype=bool)
